NSI Daily Market Report 13/01/2026
Best Regards:
MR. DEEPESH MAJIGAMKAR

From:
Nautical Supply International (NSI) DMCC <carolina@nauticalsupply.co.uk>
Sent:
13 January 2026 17:42
To:
Deepesh Majigamkar <chartering@varitasnavigation.com>
Subject:
NSI Daily Market Report 13/01/2026
Hi Deepesh
We have opened up this morning following further unrest in Iran.
The markets have focused on the rising tensions in Iran and are anticipating the risk of supply disruptions in the Middle East if the United States intervenes, which caused crude oil prices to continue climbing in early trading this morning. There\'s been nationwide protests in Iran - with reports that 500 protesters have been killed and over 10,000 arrests being made - that had initially begun due to economic grievances, but has now evolved to broader political demands. This unrest in Iran has led to a government internet blackout that\'s hindered communication across major cities, overall becoming the country\'s most serious internal crises in several years. Meanwhile, there\'s been increasing concerns over Trump following through with his promise to intervene, which he claims will be to protect peaceful protesters. After the seizure and capture of Maduro, it has become evident that Trump wont shy away from achieving his goals, despite the negative implications his actions may potentially have; due to this, US intervention is very much in the realms of possibility, which has bolstered worries over supply disruptions rather than over whether the United States will actually intervene. Iran\'s Foreign Minister has made it clear that Iran is ready for war, encouraging the US to choose "the wise option", instead of escalating the country into war. As tensions in Iran has been rising, with no indication that it will be slowing down in the near-future, the country\'s floating storage has reached record volumes over the past few weeks as Iran has been increasing the amount of oil stored at sea in an attempt to protect its output from potential supply disruptions caused by geopolitical risk. Moreover, Trump has now announced that he will be putting a 25% tariff on any country that continues to do business with Iran. As a result, this will put additional pressure on Iranian exports, followed by impacting its production, which will likely force Iran to eventually resort to its stored oil; according to data from Kpler and Vortexa, Iran has roughly 50 days of oil output on the water, since China had decreased its purchases of Iranian crude because of the US sanctions, and Iran is currently attempting to protect its stored oil from potential US military strikes. Commodity analyst at Schneider Electric, Alex Pierce, had commented that "Although Iranian authorities have claimed the situation is under control, market sentiment remains cautious... even short-term escalation could disrupt flows or increase insurance costs for shipments through the Gulf [of Hormuz]." Notably, whilst geopolitical risk continues to dominate the markets, Brent was trading 0.34% higher this morning, nearly $64.09 per barrel, and West Texas Intermediate was trading up by 0.4%, at around $59.74 per barrel, with both benchmarks almost 6% higher on the week. Geopolitical risk continues to be significantly elevated from last year and into the start of this new year despite of the ongoing bearish fundamentals and no substantial physical supply disruptions having occurred yet. Even though traders are currently focusing their attention on Iran, the capture of Maduro has sparked concerns of an oversupply, the prospect of increased Venezuelan supply returning to the market adding to the bearish sentiment as traders anticipate a significant global glut this year. We will have to see how long traders will remain focused on the Iran and whether there\'ll be any significant developments that could potentially affect oil prices.
In other news: as we all know by now, the US has continued with its blockade on Venezuelan oil flows even after the seizure and capture of Nicholas Maduro, which begun to cut off the main source of revenue for the Maduro government. The United States continues with its so called “oil quarantine” in order to control how Venezuela’s oil industry is being run and force a change in its operations whilst Maduro awaits his pending trial in New York. A few weekends ago, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio had told ABC in an interview that this oil quarantine is as far the US would go in regards to “running Venezuela”. Rubio further stated that “We have a quarantine on their oil. That means their economy will not be able to move forward until the conditions that are in the national interest of the United States and the interests of the Venezuelan people are met," adding that "So that leverage remains, that leverage is ongoing, and we expect that results here. We’re hope so – hopeful that it does – positive results for the people of Venezuela, but ultimately, most importantly, for us in the national interest of the United States”. Consequently to the continued blockade, this has led Venezuela’s state oil firm PDVSA to start reducing oil production earlier this month, on top of asking its joint ventures  - including the ones with Chevron - to also cut output, due to storage space rapidly being filled and running out. Sources that have knowledge of the operations have told Reuters that, PDVSA has asked Petrolera Sinovensa, its joint venture with China National Petroleum Corporation (CNPC), and its joint ventures with Chevron (Petropiar and Petroboscan), to reduce production by shutting off a few wells and clusters. It was also reported earlier last week that the sources had additionally mentioned that Chevron’s joint ventures haven’t started to cut their production yet, noting that Petropiar still has some storage space left. This is likely one of the reasons Chevron and its joint ventures aren’t rushing to cut production for now, on top of the fact that Chevron\'s crude continues to be loaded on tankers and transported to the United States since it is allowed to operate to and from Venezuela under a special licence given by the US Treasury. Even though fewer barrels on the market may be welcomed by some whilst the markets are filled with concerns of oversupply, reduced production and the shutting down wells is likely to add to Venezuela\'s fragile oil industry, which could be another season to put off investments towards reforming Venezuela\'s energy sector.
Also related to the US blockade, Venezuela had shipped out around a dozen oil tankers that were carrying crude and petroleum products, earlier this month. Despite, the fact that the vessels have been sanctioned by the US Treasury, they had departed Venezuela in dark mode, according to
TankerTrackers.com
cited by Reuters; it is still unclear whether these tankers had left Venezuela in defiance of the US blockade. Around that same period of time, a source that is familiar with the tanker\'s schedule had told Reuters that Venezuela had cleared at least four very large crude carriers (VLCC) to leave the country regardless of the oil blockade.
The former chief of Latin American operations at Chevron, Ali Moshiri, had told the Financial Times that his current company, Amos Global Energy Management, has discussed with institutional investors to raise $2 billion for several investment opportunities in Venezuela, which occurred prior to the recent meeting between Trump and major oil companies/traders. Initially after Maduro had been captured, statements from Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio had suggested that American oil companies would be very interested in opportunity to gain access to Venezuela\'s proven oil reserves, which is estimated at over 300 billion barrels, and to rebuild Venezuela’s oil industry. Trump had remarked that “We’re going to have our very large U.S. oil companies, the biggest anywhere in the world, go in, spend billions of dollars, fix the badly broken infrastructure, oil infrastructure, and start making money for the country”. Ali Moshiri had stated that “We have been anticipating this breakthrough for a while and our $2bn private placement memorandum is ready to go with several investment targets identified”, adding that “Interest in Venezuela has gone from zero to 99 per cent... I’ve had a dozen calls over the past 24 hours from potential investors.” However, the spark of enthusiasm that directly followed after the seizure had occurred, was later dulled as many analysts have pointed out the reality of restoring Venezuela’s oil sector, bringing to light that it would roughly take a decade and $100 billion in investments for Venezuela to be able to match its peak production levels during the mid-1970s. Moreover, the United States has recently been reconsidering whether they should directly funnel money into recovering Venezuela\'s oil industry; currently, it appears that the US is favouring towards the investments coming straight from companies and capital markets, whilst the United States provides security and stable operating environments to cushion any potential risks that comes with the large investments. This alone will potentially make investors hesitant to put money into Venezuela\'s oil, on top of the uncertainty that derives from the political and financial volatility in the South American country.
